Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»Team SA medal hope Tatjana Smith content to take things as they come

Tatjana Smith cut a relaxed figure as she counted down the days to the start of the swimming at the Paris Olympics 2024.

Tatjana Smithcut a relaxed figureas she counted down the days to the start of the Paris Olympics 2024. All the hard work has been done, having worked tirelessly with her trusted coachRocco Meiringahead of her second Games.

For the latest Paris Olympics 2024 news, bookmarkThe South African website’sdedicated section

In Tokyo three years ago she still managed to win silver in the women’s 100m breaststroke (her time of 1:04.82 came in the semi-finals) and gold in the 200m despite an interrupted schedule given the complications related to the Covid-19 pandemic as well as load shedding.

On that occasion, her debut Olympics, she said she was using the 100m as a springboard for her main event, the 200m breaststroke. It’s a tried and tested strategy, also producing a silver-gold at the 2022 Commonwealth Games, although double gold at those 2018 Games.

The 27-year-old is in Paris with a target on her back. She’s second fastest in the world over 100m at these Games and the quickest over 200m. It’s always marrying rankings with a prediction, but there’s a steely inner-belief behind that smile that has the cameras flashing.

Smith is a natural competitor, and a born racer. Pretty much as Chad le Clos is, and showed in his prime. One race at a time and get into the final. Once in the final it’s game on.

China’s Tang Qianting heads the 2024 rankings with a best of 1:04.39, with Smith third after a 1:05.41 in April. The meat in the sandwich is Russian Evgeniia Chukinova who has withdrawn from Paris, opening the way for the South African to be the Chinese swimmer’s biggest threat.

Or, is it the other way round?

Without making any predictions,all the smart money is on Smith winning two medals, which would place her with four alongside Le Clos in terms of Team SA’s Olympic roll of honour.

Smith has been her normal jovial self, although one could detect just the slightest hint of “let’s just get started” this week, which is a good thing.

That’s in the DNA of every top sports person. And, coach Meiring is always one to stand in the background and deflect attention, but he has been walking around the village with the slightest trace of a smile all week.

Almost as it saying,“Suid Afrika, hulle weet nie wat ons weet nie”.

For Smith it’s following something that has worked so well before.

She’s on the brink of becoming Team SA’s most garlanded Olympian, and only from two such Games.

“It’s a case of (trying to) perfect the small things a swimming is very technical, It’s those small margins that count, I don’t think physically we can do much more. The one difference is that you’re growing as a person and you’re changing. I’m getting older, so that’s a massive difference compared to Tokyo, but otherwise it’s just the same, trying to get it right every time.”

Over the years, the Team SA tone has been set by the swimmers. Whenever swimming has gone without a medal, as in 1992 and 2008, the return was two medals overall and one. Therefore the expectations are there for Smith to deliver in Paris. It doesn’t faze her.

“It’s a daily thing that you have to tell yourself that you’re not swimming for others’ expectations. I think sometimes my own expectations are higher than those others!, For me, I trust in God and know that His plan will ultimately work out. I can only prepare as well as I can, and whatever happens on that day happens. I always say that in Tokyo, maybe if we raced the day after, someone else could have been Olympic champion.”

You can see how humble she remains, fame and deserved fortune not going to her head. In Paris, the time schedule is different to Tokyo, with the finals being raced in the evening whereas they were in the mornings at the 2020 Game, but the format is the same.

“The 100 is always first and it’s always nice to have as a pre-race for the 200 metres. Sometimes I start enjoying it too much and then get nervous, thinking ‘how am I going survive in the 200’, but it is always good to just see where I am and have that easy speed in the beginning. You have to stay calm in this. It’s about how we react to situations and how positive we are in those situations that count. So I’m just saying positive. I know it’s going to go well, and I’ve prepared well.’

That last statement is a gold nugget of intent.

So, be prepared to see the name Smith step onto the podium in Paris, perhaps twice. Now married to Joel, she is loving life and admits she is still “getting used to seeing a very short surname on my swim cap”.

Married life suits here as well. I love that part of life. And I think it just makes you realize that swimming is only a season, and there’s different seasons, so I’m already enjoying our other seasons.

“So, yes, I’m enjoying it, and I’m excited to race for the first time at this level, my first Olympics as a Smith.”

Gary Lemkeis reporting live from Paris for the Team SA website for the duration of the Paris Olympics 2024

title: Team SA medal hope Tatjana Smith to take things as they come
author: Garrin Lambley
description: Tatjana Smith cut a relaxed figure as she counted down the days to the start of the swimming at the Paris Olympics 2024.
